SUNNY_DAYLIGHT.rationale: f/16 maximizes depth of field for fore-to-background sharpness. Base ISO for maximum dynamic range.
SUNNY_DAYLIGHT.proTip: Use the hyperfocal distance to ensure maximum sharpness from near to infinity. Focus 1/3 into the scene.
SUNNY_DAYLIGHT.commonMistake: Setting focus to infinity misses the foreground in a wide landscape shot.

CLOUDY_DAY.rationale: Clouds diffuse light evenly, reducing contrast so you can open up 1 stop from f/16 while maintaining sharpness.
CLOUDY_DAY.proTip: Overcast days are perfect for waterfalls and forests where harsh shadows would be distracting.
CLOUDY_DAY.commonMistake: Including a large expanse of white overcast sky overwhelms the scene—keep it to 1/3 or less.

OVERCAST_DAY.rationale: Heavy overcast reduces light enough to warrant f/8. Still maximizes depth of field while increasing shutter speed.
OVERCAST_DAY.proTip: Add a CPL filter to cut glare on water and foliage, increasing color saturation on dull days.
OVERCAST_DAY.commonMistake: Not using a circular polarizer loses 2 stops but dramatically improves color saturation.

SHADE_OR_HEAVY_OVERCAST.rationale: Very low ambient light requires bump to ISO 200 and wider aperture. IBIS helps maintain sharp handheld shots.
SHADE_OR_HEAVY_OVERCAST.proTip: Shade can provide beautiful, moody light for atmospheric landscapes. Embrace the moodiness.
SHADE_OR_HEAVY_OVERCAST.commonMistake: Underexposing in shade and boosting in post introduces excessive noise.

GOLDEN_HOUR_TRIPOD.rationale: Tripod enables slow shutter at low ISO. Manual mode prevents the camera from changing exposure as light changes rapidly.
GOLDEN_HOUR_TRIPOD.proTip: Use the 2-second self-timer or remote shutter to eliminate camera shake when pressing the shutter button.
GOLDEN_HOUR_TRIPOD.commonMistake: Touching the camera during exposure causes blur even with a tripod—use a remote or timer.

WATERFALL_LONG_EXPOSURE.rationale: 0.5s gives silky water effect. ND filter required in daylight to prevent overexposure at these settings.
WATERFALL_LONG_EXPOSURE.proTip: Experiment between 0.5s and 4s. Very long exposures create cotton-candy water; 0.5s preserves more movement detail.
WATERFALL_LONG_EXPOSURE.commonMistake: Not using an ND filter in daylight overexposes the shot at 0.5s even at f/16 and base ISO.

TWILIGHT_HANDHELD.rationale: Twilight window is brief. Handheld allows quick repositioning. ISO 800 and f/4 balance quality vs. light.
TWILIGHT_HANDHELD.proTip: IBIS is very helpful here—it adds 3-5 stops of shake compensation, letting you go down to 1/15s or slower.
TWILIGHT_HANDHELD.commonMistake: Waiting too long in the twilight window—light changes fast; bracket exposures every few minutes.

NIGHT_CITYSCAPE_TRIPOD.rationale: 5s exposure captures light trails from moving vehicles. Small aperture creates starburst on city lights.
NIGHT_CITYSCAPE_TRIPOD.proTip: Wait for traffic to pass at key intersections to capture full light trails from cars and buses.
NIGHT_CITYSCAPE_TRIPOD.commonMistake: Using f/22 for star effect reduces sharpness from diffraction—f/11 is the sweet spot.

SNOWY_SCENE_SUNNY.rationale: Bright snow reflects massive light. Use +1 to +1.5 EV exposure compensation so snow renders white, not grey.
SNOWY_SCENE_SUNNY.proTip: Add +1.5 EV compensation. The camera meters snow as middle grey by default, creating underexposed images.
SNOWY_SCENE_SUNNY.commonMistake: Trusting evaluative metering without compensation results in dark, grey-looking snow.

FOGGY_SCENE.rationale: Fog reduces contrast and diffuses light. These settings handle the reduced light while retaining detail.
FOGGY_SCENE.proTip: Look for layers of depth—foreground rocks, middle trees, background mountains fading into the fog.
FOGGY_SCENE.commonMistake: Adding contrast in post destroys the dreamy, ethereal quality that makes fog images compelling.

DESERT_BRIGHT_DAY.rationale: Extreme brightness in desert conditions. CPL filter removes sky haze and deepens blue sky color.
DESERT_BRIGHT_DAY.proTip: Shoot in the golden hours—midday desert light is harsh with no shadow interest. Dawn or dusk are magical.
DESERT_BRIGHT_DAY.commonMistake: Shooting midday removes all texture from dunes—the extreme overhead angle creates flat, shadowed surfaces.

BEACH_BRIGHT_DAY.rationale: Water and sand reflect a lot of light. Fast shutter freezes wave movement while maintaining landscape depth.
BEACH_BRIGHT_DAY.proTip: Use a circular polarizer to eliminate glare from the water and make the ocean deep blue.
BEACH_BRIGHT_DAY.commonMistake: Not using a CPL filter leaves a white glare on the water surface, losing all color and depth.

MOUNTAIN_CLEAR_DAY.rationale: Clear mountain air increases UV at altitude—a UV/sky filter helps. f/11 maximizes foreground to summit sharpness.
MOUNTAIN_CLEAR_DAY.proTip: Include a strong foreground element (wildflowers, rocks) to add depth and scale to mountain shots.
MOUNTAIN_CLEAR_DAY.commonMistake: Shooting without a foreground element creates a postcard-flat image with no sense of depth.

NIGHT_STARSCAPE.rationale: 500 Rule: 500/24mm focal length = ~20s max before star trailing. Wide aperture and high ISO maximize light capture.
NIGHT_STARSCAPE.proTip: Use live view at 10x magnification focused on a bright star to achieve precise manual focus.
NIGHT_STARSCAPE.commonMistake: Autofocus fails in the dark—always use manual focus set to slightly inside infinity for stars.

BLUE_HOUR_CITY.rationale: Blue hour gives perfect sky-to-city-light balance. Long exposure smooths water and blurs traffic.
BLUE_HOUR_CITY.proTip: Shoot horizontal and stitch panoramas during the 10-minute blue hour window for maximum impact.
BLUE_HOUR_CITY.commonMistake: Arriving too late after sunset misses the brief blue hour window when sky and artificial lights balance perfectly.

FOREST_DIFFUSED_LIGHT.rationale: Forest canopy creates dappled, uneven light. f/5.6 balances depth of field vs. shutter speed.
FOREST_DIFFUSED_LIGHT.proTip: Look for misty mornings after rain—ground fog adds mystery and depth to forest landscapes.
FOREST_DIFFUSED_LIGHT.commonMistake: Shooting midday creates harsh dappled light that's difficult to expose correctly.

AUTUMN_COLOR.rationale: Base ISO for maximum color fidelity. Overcast light is actually ideal for autumn—no harsh shadows.
AUTUMN_COLOR.proTip: Use a CPL filter to saturate autumn colors and eliminate glare from wet leaves.
AUTUMN_COLOR.commonMistake: Shooting in direct midday sun creates harsh shadows that diminish the warmth of autumn foliage.

BLACK_AND_WHITE_LANDSCAPE.rationale: Shoot RAW for full tonal control. Strong directional light creates compelling shadows in monochrome.
BLACK_AND_WHITE_LANDSCAPE.proTip: Use a red filter (or simulate in post) to darken blue skies dramatically and make clouds pop.
BLACK_AND_WHITE_LANDSCAPE.commonMistake: Converting overcast, flat-light shots to B&W results in low-contrast, muddy grey images.

REFLECTIONS_LANDSCAPE.rationale: Early morning calm water gives perfect mirror reflections. Shoot just after sunrise before wind disturbs the surface.
REFLECTIONS_LANDSCAPE.proTip: Get low to the waterline for a symmetrical composition that mirrors sky and landscape perfectly.
REFLECTIONS_LANDSCAPE.commonMistake: Arriving after morning wind picks up destroys mirror-still water reflections.

STORM_LIGHTNING.rationale: Bulb or 30s exposure in the dark means any lightning bolt that fires during the exposure registers automatically. ISO 100 because lightning is extremely bright — the bolt itself provides the light.
STORM_LIGHTNING.proTip: Point into the storm cell, not directly at individual lightning. Use a cable release or intervalometer. In light-polluted skies, narrow to f/16 to avoid overexposing the background.
STORM_LIGHTNING.commonMistake: Using autofocus in the dark — the camera hunts and misses the shot entirely. Set manual focus to infinity before the storm arrives, then lock it.

TIME_LAPSE_SETUP.rationale: The 180-degree shutter rule: shutter speed = double the frame interval. For 25fps playback with 1-second intervals, 1/50s is correct. ND filter required in daylight to achieve 1/50s. Manual mode is mandatory — any aperture variation causes flicker.
TIME_LAPSE_SETUP.proTip: Deflicker in post using LRTimelapse or DaVinci Resolve. Shoot RAW for maximum dynamic range. Use ND8-ND64 to achieve 1/50s in bright daylight.
TIME_LAPSE_SETUP.commonMistake: Using aperture priority — any aperture micro-shift between frames causes visible flicker in the final video. Manual mode only, every time.

SNOWY_SCENE.rationale: Snow reflects ~90% of light and fools evaluative metering into underexposing — the camera renders snow as grey. Set +1 to +1.5EV exposure compensation to restore white snow.
SNOWY_SCENE.proTip: Use spot or centre-weighted metering on a mid-tone in the scene (a coat, bare tree bark) instead of the snow itself. Or set evaluative metering with +1.3EV compensation.
SNOWY_SCENE.commonMistake: Trusting evaluative metering on snow — the image will look grey and underexposed. Always apply positive exposure compensation in snowy scenes.
